stem: For which value of \( t \) are the vectors \( \mathbf{v}_1 = (1,\ 0,\ [[a]]) \), \( \mathbf{v}_2 = (0,\ 1,\ [[b]]) \), and \( \mathbf{v}_3 = ([[p]],\ [[q]],\ t) \) linearly dependent?
solution: The three vectors are dependent exactly when \( \det[\mathbf{v}_1\ \mathbf{v}_2\ \mathbf{v}_3] = 0 \). Expanding gives \( t - ([[a]])([[p]]) - ([[b]])([[q]]) = 0 \), so \( t = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This subtracts the two products instead of adding them.
This pairs the coefficients with the wrong components.
This adds only \( [[a]] \) and \( [[b]] \), ignoring \( [[p]] \) and \( [[q]] \).
This has the wrong sign.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This uses only the first product term.
This uses only the second product term.

stem: For what value of \( t \) are the vectors \( \mathbf{v}_1 = ([[a]],\ [[b]]) \) and \( \mathbf{v}_2 = ([[c]],\ t) \) in \( \mathbb{R}^2 \) linearly dependent (i.e., parallel)?
solution: Two vectors in \( \mathbb{R}^2 \) are dependent exactly when their \( 2\times2 \) determinant vanishes: \( [[a]]\cdot t - [[b]]\cdot[[c]] = 0 \), so \( t = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds instead of solving the determinant equation for \( t \).
This has the wrong sign.
This multiplies the two known coordinates instead of solving the determinant equation.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This just copies \( \mathbf{v}_2 \)'s known coordinate.

stem: The vectors \( \mathbf{v}_1 = ([[a1]], [[a2]], [[a3]]) \) and \( \mathbf{v}_2 = ([[b1]], [[b2]], [[b3]]) \) are linearly independent. For what value of \( t \) does \( \mathbf{w} = ([[c1]], [[c2]], t) \) lie in the span of \( \mathbf{v}_1 \) and \( \mathbf{v}_2 \) (equivalently, for what \( t \) is \( \{\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \mathbf{w}\} \) linearly dependent)?
solution: Solve \( x\mathbf{v}_1 + y\mathbf{v}_2 \) matching the first two coordinates of \( \mathbf{w} \): \( [[a1]]x+[[b1]]y=[[c1]] \), \( [[a2]]x+[[b2]]y=[[c2]] \). The third coordinate must then equal \( [[a3]]x+[[b3]]y = [[answer]] \), the required value of \( t \).
distractor_rationales: This just adds the two known coordinates of \( \mathbf{w} \), ignoring \( \mathbf{v}_1,\mathbf{v}_2 \) entirely.
This adds the third coordinates of \( \mathbf{v}_1 \) and \( \mathbf{v}_2 \) directly, without solving for the combination weights \( x,y \).
This combines the wrong quantities instead of first solving the \( 2\times2 \) system for \( x,y \).
This has the wrong sign.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.

stem: Which of the following is a value of \( t \) for which the vectors \( \mathbf{v}_1 = ([[a]],\ t,\ 0) \), \( \mathbf{v}_2 = (t,\ [[a]],\ 0) \), and \( \mathbf{v}_3 = (0,\ 0,\ [[b]]) \) are linearly dependent?
solution: The determinant is \( \det[\mathbf{v}_1\ \mathbf{v}_2\ \mathbf{v}_3] = [[b]]\big(([[a]])^2 - t^2\big) \), which vanishes exactly when \( t = [[a]] \) or \( t = -([[a]]) \); one such value is \( t = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is off by one from one of the two correct values.
This is off by one from one of the two correct values.
This doubles the parameter instead of solving \( t^2=[[a]]^2 \).
This assumes the vectors are dependent for \( t=0 \), which is false since \( [[a]]\ne0 \).
This confuses the unrelated coefficient \( [[b]] \) with a solution for \( t \).
This adds the two given constants instead of solving the equation.
This is off by one from the negative correct value.
This multiplies the two given constants instead of solving the equation.
